1: A revelation of God: "for he is good; his steadfast love endures forever" — the foundational covenant-affirmation. In Ephesians 2:4-7, Paul's account of salvation begins with precisely this: "But God, who is rich in mercy, because of his great love for us..." The hesed praised in this doxology is the very attribute expressed definitively in Christ's self-giving. The goodness of God toward the undeserving is the theme of both Psalm 106 and the Gospel.
2: A theme: "who is able to speak all the LORD's mighty deeds?" — the question implies the answer is no mere human. John 1:18 provides it: "No one has ever seen God, but the one and only Son, who is himself God and is in closest relationship with the Father, has made him known." Christ is the one who definitively declares the Father's mighty deeds because he both performs them and is himself their content (John 5:19-20).
3: A theme: "blessed are those who uphold justice and do what is right at every moment" — Matthew 5:6 echoes this beatitude form: "blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they will be satisfied." Christ both perfectly embodies this blessing (Matthew 3:15: "fulfill all righteousness") and gives it as a gift: 1 Corinthians 1:30 says Christ "has become for us... our righteousness." The one who blesses is the one who fulfills what the blessing requires.
4: A direct connection: "remember me, O LORD, when you show favor to your people" — the same plea appears on the dying thief's lips: "Jesus, remember me when you come into your kingdom" (Luke 23:42). Christ's response — "today you will be with me in paradise" — is the divine answer. He is the one through whom YHWH "shows favor"; the petitioner who prays to be remembered is answered in Christ who carries the petitioner into his own kingdom and life.
5: A theme: "so that I may see the well-being of your chosen ones, share the joy of your nation" — the petition to be included among the covenant people. Ephesians 1:4-5 says believers were "chosen in Christ before the foundation of the world" and "predestined for adoption as sons through Jesus Christ." The identity that was Israel's by birth becomes available to all through union with Christ — "the Chosen One" (Luke 23:35) who includes others in his election.
6: A direct connection: "we have sinned, just as our fathers did" — universal sin across generations. Romans 3:23 ("all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God") and Romans 5:12 expand this generational confession to all humanity. Christ is the one who takes on the full weight of this accumulated sin: "God made him who had no sin to be sin for us, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God" (2 Corinthians 5:21). The psalm's confession is precisely what Christ bears.
7: A revelation of God: "they did not remember your abundant steadfast love" — the failure of memory that leads to covenant failure. Jesus institutes the Eucharist as the direct remedy: "do this in remembrance of me" (1 Corinthians 11:24-25). The Last Supper creates a perpetual memory-practice for the covenant community — the answer to Israel's forgetfulness is a covenant meal designed to keep Christ's saving act in perpetual, enacted memory.
8: A direct connection: "but he saved them for the sake of his own name, to demonstrate his mighty power" — God's salvation is for the honor of his name. John 17:6 ("I have revealed your name") and Psalms 12:28 ("Father, glorify your name!") show Christ understanding his mission in these terms. Romans 3:25-26 argues that God presented Christ as a propitiation "to demonstrate his righteousness." Salvation accomplishes both rescue and divine self-vindication.
9: A type: "he rebuked the Red Sea and it dried up; he led them through the depths as through a desert" — 1 Corinthians 10:1-2 interprets the sea-crossing as a baptismal type: "they were all baptized into Moses in the cloud and in the sea." Baptism into Christ (Romans 6:3-4) is the fulfillment: passing through death-waters to new life. The Red Sea is the precursor of the cross — the people pass through the waters of judgment because the road has been opened by divine power over chaos.
10: A type: "he saved them from the hand of the one who hated them and redeemed them from the power of the enemy" — the Exodus as redemption from enemy power. Colossians 1:13 uses the same language: "he has rescued us from the dominion of darkness." Hebrews 2:14-15 says Christ shared in flesh and blood "so that by his death he might break the power of him who holds the power of death — that is, the devil." The enemy is personalized and the rescue is through death itself.
11: A shadow: "the waters covered their enemies; not one of them remained" — the destruction of Pharaoh's army. Revelation 19:11-21 draws on Exodus imagery for the final destruction of the beast and his armies. The eschatological "sea giving up its dead" (Revelation 20:13) and the beast cast into the lake of fire complete the trajectory of this divine military victory. Christ as the cosmic warrior of Revelation 19:11-16 is the fulfillment of this total divine victory.
12: A theme: "then they believed his words and sang his praise" — faith responding to salvation in song. This is the pattern of Gospel response. The Song of Moses (Exod 15) is the template for Revelation's "song of Moses and of the Lamb" (Revelation 15:3-4) — the redeemed singing their salvation. Faith, praise, and song are the response of those saved by Christ, replicating the pattern established at the sea.
13: A revelation of God: "but soon they forgot what he had done and did not wait for his counsel" — forgetting as the root of covenant failure. The Eucharist is Christ's direct response to this pattern: "do this in remembrance of me" (1 Corinthians 11:24-25). The Last Supper creates a perpetual memory-practice for the covenant community — directly addressing the forgetfulness the psalm laments. Covenant memory is constitutive of covenant faithfulness.
14: A type: "they craved intensely in the wilderness and put God to the test in the desert" — the wilderness testing of Israel is what Jesus retraced and reversed. Matthew 4:1-11 places Jesus in the wilderness where he is tested at precisely the points where Israel failed: bread, testing God, idolatry. Hebrews 4:15 confirms: "we have a high priest... tempted in every way, just as we are — yet was without sin." Israel failed the wilderness test; Christ passed it, accomplishing what Israel could not.
15: A revelation of God: "he gave them what they asked for but sent a wasting disease into their lives" — the tragic dynamic of getting what you demand apart from God's true provision. John 6:32-35 contrasts the craved manna with Christ himself: "my Father gives you the true bread from heaven... I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me will never go hungry." The craving for inferior provision contrasts with the satisfaction Christ provides — the one who alone truly satisfies.
16: A shadow: "in the camp they were envious of Moses, Aaron the holy one of the LORD" — envy directed at God's appointed leaders. Matthew 27:18 identifies envy as the motive behind Christ's crucifixion: "he knew it was out of envy that they had handed Jesus over." The envious rejection of God's chosen mediators is the persistent human pattern; in Christ this pattern reaches its ultimate and most paradoxically redemptive expression — the envied one crucified becomes the source of life for those who crucified him.
17: A revelation of God: "the earth opened and swallowed up Dathan, the group of Abiram closed over them" — divine judgment on those who rebel against YHWH's appointed mediators. Hebrews 10:29 warns against treating lightly "the blood of the covenant" and "insulting the Spirit of grace" — the judgment for rejecting Christ, the ultimate mediator, is more severe than the Korah-judgment. The pattern of judgment on mediator-rejecters is not cancelled in the NT but intensified.
18: A revelation of God: "fire broke out in their company, the flame consumed the wicked" — divine fire consuming covenant rebels. Hebrews 12:29 applies this: "our God is a consuming fire." The same fire that consumed Korah's company is the eschatological fire Christ speaks of (Matthew 25:41). Those in Christ are not consumed because he has borne the fire at Calvary (Isaiah 43:2: "when you walk through fire, you will not be burned... for I am the LORD your God").
19: A type: "at Horeb they made a calf and bowed down to a metal idol" — the golden calf as paradigmatic idolatry. 1 Corinthians 10:7 applies this: "do not be idolaters, as some of them were." Positively, Christ is "the image of the invisible God" (Colossians 1:15) — the true "form" of God that Israel sought to manufacture. What Israel tried to create in gold was the divine image already present in the Son; the idol replaces the image that was always waiting to be revealed in the Incarnation.
20: A direct connection: "they traded the glory of God for the image of a grass-eating ox" — Romans 1:23 quotes this language to describe universal human idolatry: "they exchanged the glory of the immortal God for images." This exchange is the root of all human corruption (Romans 1:18-25). Christ is "the image of the invisible God" (Colossians 1:15; 2 Corinthians 4:4) — the one in whom the glory that humans have traded away is restored and made visible. Beholding Christ reverses the idolatrous exchange.
21: A revelation of God: "they forgot God their Savior" — YHWH as mōšîaʿ (Savior) forgotten by his own people. Matthew 1:21 names Jesus (Yēšûaʿ = YHWH saves) as the Savior who comes to his people; Luke 2:11 ("a Savior has been born to you"). The people who forgot the Exodus-Savior now encounter him incarnate — the name Jesus itself carries the Exodus-rescue tradition forward, declaring in every use that YHWH has come to save.
22: A revelation of God: "wonderful things in the land of Ham and awesome deeds by the Red Sea" — recitation of historical mighty acts. The Christological weight is cumulative: every act of divine rescue and power in the Exodus is a type pointing to the definitive rescue. Hebrews 9:11-12 presents Christ's work as achieving what the entire Exodus-and-Tabernacle pattern could only preview: "eternal redemption" accomplished "once for all" by his own blood.
23: A direct connection: "Moses, his chosen one, stood in the gap before him and turned his anger away from destroying them" — the OT's most concentrated image of intercessory mediation. The NT applies the gap-standing to Christ with extraordinary clarity: 1 Timothy 2:5 ("one mediator between God and mankind, the man Christ Jesus"); Hebrews 7:25 ("he always lives to intercede for them"); Ezekiel 22:30 lamented that no one could "stand in the gap" — the gap Christ fills permanently through his intercession, based not on prayer alone but on his own shed blood (Hebrews 9:24; Psalms 12:24).
24: A direct connection: "then they despised the pleasant land; they did not trust his word" — the Kadesh-barnea unbelief. Hebrews 3:7–4:11 applies this directly to faith in Christ: "since the promise of entering his rest still stands, let us be careful that none of you be found to have fallen short of it" (Psalms 4:1). The rest Israel failed to enter through unbelief is available through Christ; he is himself the Sabbath-rest (Hebrews 4:9-10: "there remains a Sabbath-rest for the people of God").
25: A revelation of God: "they grumbled in their tents and did not listen to the LORD's voice" — grumbling as covenantal hardness. In contrast, John 8:29 ("I always do what pleases him") and Hebrews 10:7 (Christ: "I have come to do your will, my God") present Jesus as the obedient Son who never grumbles. Christ's perfect obedience is what Israel's grumbling failure required — one who listened and obeyed in the very place where Israel refused.
26: A direct connection: "so he swore with uplifted hand that he would make them fall in the wilderness" — the divine oath of exclusion from rest. Hebrews 4:3 cites this oath: "as I declared on oath in my anger, 'They shall never enter my rest.'" The oath stands against unbelief. But the same God who swore this oath also swore regarding Christ: "you are a priest forever, in the order of Melchizedek" (Psalms 110:4; Hebrews 7:21). The oath of exclusion is answered by the oath of priestly provision — Christ opens the rest that unbelief had closed.
27: A shadow: "and disperse their descendants among the nations, scattering them through the lands" — exile as covenant curse. John 11:52 says Christ died "to gather together the scattered children of God." Ephesians 2:11-22 describes the ingathering: Gentiles "far off" brought near "through the blood of Christ." The scattering of exile — the covenant curse — is reversed through the cross, which creates one new humanity from the scattered peoples.
28: A shadow: "they joined themselves to Baal-peor and ate sacrifices offered to the dead" — eating at the table of false gods. 1 Corinthians 10:20-21 applies this directly: "the sacrifices of pagans are offered to demons... you cannot have a part in both the Lord's table and the table of demons." The contrast between the Lord's Supper and the Baal-peor meals is explicit in Paul. Christ's table displaces every table of idols as the place of true communion — the living Christ against the sacrifices offered to the dead.
29: A shadow: "they aroused his anger with their practices, and plague broke out among them" — sin leading to divine judgment. Isaiah 53:4 says of the Servant: "surely he took up our pain and bore our suffering... yet we considered him punished by God, stricken by him, and afflicted." The word rendered "stricken" carries resonances of divine plague-striking. Christ bears the plague-judgment on Israel's behalf — the one on whom it finally fell so that it need not fall on his people (1 Corinthians 5:7: "our Passover lamb").
30: A shadow: "then Phinehas stepped forward and executed judgment, and the plague was stopped" — one decisive priestly action stopping consuming judgment. The pattern of a single person's action stopping divine wrath points toward Christ's atoning work, though Phinehas's means (violent execution) contrasts with Christ's (his own death). Hebrews 9:26 ("he appeared to do away with sin by the sacrifice of himself") is the fulfillment: the one who steps forward to end the plague does so by becoming the offering — the atonement accomplished from within rather than imposed from without.
31: A direct connection: "and it was reckoned to him as righteousness for all generations, forever" — the ḥāšab lĕṣĕdāqâ (reckoned/credited as righteousness) vocabulary. Romans 4:3 applies the same formula to Abraham; Romans 5:18-19 extends it: "through the one man's obedience many will be made righteous." The forensic reckoning-of-righteousness that appears here and in Genesis 15:6 becomes the technical language of justification by faith in Christ (Romans 3:21-26; Psalms 4:24-25). The pattern of credited righteousness anticipates the imputed righteousness of Christ.
32: A shadow: "they angered him at the waters of Meribah, and it went badly for Moses because of them" — Moses excluded from Canaan due to the people's sin. Moses's exclusion because of the people's rebellion is a somber shadow of Christ bearing consequences for the people's failures. But the contrast is more illuminating: Moses was excluded from the promised land; Christ enters the final promised land as the forerunner (Hebrews 6:20), bringing his people with him where the greatest human mediator was barred.
33: A revelation of God: "for they rebelled against his Spirit" — rebellion against the Spirit of God is a persistent Israelite pattern. Acts 7:51 charges the Sanhedrin: "you always resist the Holy Spirit!" Matthew 12:31-32 identifies blasphemy against the Holy Spirit as the unforgivable sin. The Spirit who was provoked at Meribah (Isaiah 63:10-11) is the same Spirit poured out at Pentecost (Acts 2) — Israel's historical rebellion against the Spirit is the backdrop for the NT's urgent warnings against grieving and resisting him.
34: A revelation of God: "they did not destroy the peoples the LORD had commanded them to" — incomplete obedience is the failure-pattern catalogued throughout the psalm. Matthew 3:15 — "it is proper for us to do this to fulfill all righteousness" — presents Christ as the one who fulfills what Israel consistently failed to fulfill. Christ completes the divine mandate without remainder; his obedience is total where Israel's was always partial (Romans 5:19: "through the obedience of the one man many will be made righteous").
35: A theme: "they mingled with the nations and learned their ways" — syncretistic assimilation. Romans 12:2 addresses the same temptation: "do not conform to the pattern of this world, but be transformed by the renewing of your mind." In Christ the relationship to the nations is transformed: the goal is not separation from the nations but witness to them (Matthew 28:19-20) — not assimilation but incarnational presence. Christ creates a community that is "in the world but not of the world" (John 17:14-16).
36: A theme: "they served their idols, which became a snare to them" — the enslaving power of idolatry. Galatians 4:8-9 describes pre-Christ existence as "slavery to those who by nature are not gods." Christ frees from this slavery: "if the Son sets you free, you will be free indeed" (John 8:36). The snare of idolatry is broken by the one whose yoke is easy and whose burden is light (Matthew 11:29-30) — the service that liberates rather than enslaves.
37: A shadow: "they sacrificed their sons and daughters to demons" — child sacrifice to Molech, the extreme consequence of idolatry. The contrast with Christ is starkest here: God does not demand the sacrifice of children; God himself provides the sacrifice — his own Son — so that no more blood need be shed. Genesis 22:8 ("God himself will provide the lamb") and John 3:16 ("God so loved the world that he gave his one and only Son") answer the demonic sacrifice with the divine gift.
38: A shadow: "they shed innocent blood, the blood of their sons and daughters... and the land was polluted with blood" — innocent blood polluting the land. Matthew 27:4 (Judas: "I have betrayed innocent blood") and Psalms 27:24-25 (Pilate washing his hands) frame Christ's death in these terms. But unlike the innocent blood that pollutes, Christ's blood cleanses: "the blood of Jesus, his Son, purifies us from all sin" (1 John 1:7; Hebrews 9:14). The pollution-through-blood becomes purification-through-blood — the ultimate reversal.
39: A theme: "they thus defiled themselves by their deeds and played the harlot with their practices" — covenant unfaithfulness as spiritual adultery. Ephesians 5:25-32 presents the marriage metaphor: Christ is the Husband who "loved the church and gave himself up for her to make her holy, cleansing her by the washing with water through the word." The faithless bride does not render the husband faithless; Christ loves and cleanses the unfaithful bride — the ultimate expression of the steadfast love the psalm's opening praised.
40: A direct connection: "then the LORD's anger blazed against his own people" — divine wrath against the covenant people. Romans 3:25 says God presented Christ as a "hilastērion (propitiation) through faith in his blood" — propitiation is the satisfaction of divine wrath. Christ absorbs the burning anger that centuries of covenant violation accumulated. 1 John 2:2 says Christ "is the propitiation for our sins, and not for ours only but also for the sins of the whole world." The blazing anger of Psalms 106:40 is quenched in Christ.
41: A shadow: "he handed them over to the nations, and those who hated them ruled over them" — subjugation as covenant consequence. The ultimate reversal is Christ's kingdom: "the kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Messiah, and he will reign forever and ever" (Revelation 11:15). Those who were "handed over" to hostile rulers now reign with Christ (Revelation 5:10); those who mocked and ruled become subject to the one they condemned (Philippians 2:10-11).
42: A shadow: "their enemies oppressed them and brought them under their power" — subjugation and powerlessness. 1 Corinthians 15:24-27 says Christ "must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet... the last enemy to be destroyed is death." Every form of oppressive domination in the biblical narrative — Pharaoh, Babylon, Rome — anticipates the final enemy. Christ's resurrection is the decisive victory over all subjugating power, the beginning of the end of all oppression.
43: A type: "many times he delivered them, but they were rebellious... and sank low in their iniquity" — the judges-cycle of rebellion, oppression, cry, deliverance, repeated endlessly. Each act of deliverance types Christ's definitive deliverance. Christ breaks the cycle permanently: "Christ died to sin once for all" (Romans 6:10). The "many times" of the judges anticipates the "once for all" of Calvary (Hebrews 9:26-28; Psalms 10:10) — Christ does not repeat the cycle but ends it.
44: A revelation of God: "but he took note of their distress when he heard their cry" — the God who hears the cry of the distressed. This is the Exodus pattern (Exodus 3:7). Christ enters into human distress as a participant: "we do not have a high priest who is unable to empathize with our weaknesses" (Hebrews 4:15). The God who hears becomes the God who suffers alongside — and the God who delivers from within the suffering rather than from above it.
45: A direct connection: "he remembered his covenant for their sake and relented according to his great steadfast love" — covenant-memory and hesed as the basis of every deliverance. Luke 22:20 ("this cup is the new covenant in my blood") is the ultimate covenant-remembrance: God does not merely recall the covenant but seals it with Christ's blood. The hesed that drove each judges-cycle deliverance is "expressed fully" (Titus 3:4-5) in Christ's self-giving on the cross.
46: A revelation of God: "he caused them to be pitied by all their captors" — God softening enemies' hearts toward captives. Joseph in Egypt, Daniel in Babylon — God grants favor in enemy territory. Christ goes further: "Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing" (Luke 23:34) — not just softening captors but praying for executioners. Divine compassion toward the captive reaches its furthest expression in the cross, where the captive prays for his captors.
47: A direct connection: "save us, O LORD our God, and bring us back from among the nations" — the petition for eschatological ingathering. John 11:52 says Christ died "to bring together the scattered children of God." Ephesians 2:11-22: Gentiles "far off" brought near "through the blood of Christ." The final praise the psalm anticipates — "giving thanks to your holy name" — is the worship of the redeemed from every nation: "a great multitude that no one could count, from every nation, tribe, people and language" (Revelation 7:9-10).
48: A direct connection: "Blessed be the LORD, the God of Israel, from everlasting to everlasting! And let all the people say, 'Amen.' Praise the LORD!" — the closing doxology of Book IV. Philippians 2:10-11 is its fulfillment: "at the name of Jesus every knee should bow... and every tongue acknowledge that Jesus Christ is Lord, to the glory of God the Father." The universal "Amen" that the psalm calls for from "all the people" is the eschatological worship of the Lamb (Revelation 5:13-14; Psalms 19:4) — the end to which all Scripture's praise-calls finally point.
